text: The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the gastrocolic ligament to mobilize the transverse colon inferiorly. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg to facilitate exposure. The omental adhesions to the gallbladder and liver are freed, continuing the dissection laterally until the hepatic flexure is fully mobilized.

The surgeon uses prograsp forceps to retract the duodenum upwards, freeing its lateral attachments and carefully dissecting it away from the inferior vena cava. The robotic system facilitates precise dissection in the confined space.

The surgeon localizes the ampulla, identifying the large growth through tactile feedback. A duodenotomy is performed on the lateral wall using an ultrasonic energy device, then extended longitudinally with hook electrocautery, ensuring the incision is made at the ampulla level to avoid excessive length.

The surgeon pulls the ampulla through the duodenotomy, placing stay sutures to keep the duodenal wall open for retraction. Ampullary transduction begins in the inframedial aspect using an ultrasonic energy device, deepening the dissection. Pancreatic juice flows from a dilated pancreatic duct, and a stitch is placed between the pancreatic duct and duodenal mucosa to prevent retraction and aid traction. The dissection continues superiorly, with visible bile flow from the bile duct.

The surgeon observes a dilated bile duct and pancreatic duct. The specimen is placed in an endo bag and removed for a frozen section biopsy. Following a negative biopsy result for high-grade dysplasia or invasive malignancy, the procedure continues with the ampulectomy.

The surgeon begins the reconstruction of the common bile duct (CBD) by suturing the superior aspect of the CBD to the duodenal mucosa using PDS-C-C sutures. A bridging anastomosis is performed between the CBD and the pancreatic duct.

The surgeon uses a robotic approach for transduodenal ampullectomy, placing stay sutures over the duodenum to assist in closing the duodenotomy. A blue EndoGIS stapler is used for closure, with the duodenotomy being closed transversely to prevent stricture formation.
A surgical drain is placed in the right subhepatic space, and the omentum is positioned over the duodenotomy site.
